---
title: "American Black Elderberry (Sambucus canadensis)"
slug: "sambucus-canadensis"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/257738959/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "American Black Elderberry"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Dipsacales"
tax_family: "Viburnaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Dipsacales", "Viburnaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/257738959/medium.jpg" alt="American Black Elderberry" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/laurenbutlerr">
laurenbutlerr</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/149560976">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Dipsacales
-   **Family:** Viburnaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=84300)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common across Florida and found in moist, disturbed soils, along roadsides, and in wetlands.

## Natural history notes

A deciduous shrub characterized by its compound leaves, flat clusters of small white flowers, and dark purple to black berries. In Florida, it is commonly found in moist habitats such as wetlands, floodplains, and along roadsides. It blooms in late spring to summer, and its fruits provide an important food source for birds and other wildlife.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/84300-Sambucus-canadensis)
-   [Local profile](https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-sambucus-nigra-subsp-canadensis/)
